coredata.dc:title: Effect of a new slow-release zinc fertilizer based on carbon dots on the zinc concentration, growth indices, and yield in wheat (Triticum aestivum)

Mirbolook, Atena
Sadeghi, Jalal
Lakzian, Amir
coredata.dc:description: The present study aimed to introduce a new carbon dots nanocarrier (Zn-NCDs) as a slow-release Zn fertilizer. Zn-NCDs was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and characterized by instrumental methods. A greenhouse experiment was then conducted involving two Zn sources (Zn-NCDs and ZnSO4), three concentrations of Zn-NCDs (2, 4, and 8 mg/L), and under sand culture conditions. This study comprehensively evaluated the effects of Zn-NCDs on the zinc, nitrogen, and phytic acid content, biomass, growth indices, and yield in bread wheat (cv. Sirvan). Also, a fluorescence microscope was used to examine the in vivo transport route of Zn-NCDs in wheat organs. Finally, the availability of Zn in soil samples treated with Zn-NCDs was evaluated over 30 days in an incubation experiment. The findings indicated that Zn-NCDs as a slow-release fertilizer increased root-shoot biomass, fertile spikelet, and grain yield by 20, 44, 16, and 43%, respectively, compared to ZnSO4 treatment. The concentration of zinc and nitrogen in the grain was increased by 19% and 118%, respectively, while phytic acid was decreased by 18% than ZnSO4 treatment. Microscopic observations revealed that wheat plants could absorb and transfer Zn-NCDs from roots to stems and leaves through vascular bundles. This study demonstrated for the first time that Zn-NCDs could be used as a slow-release Zn fertilizer with high efficiency and low cost in wheat enrichment. In addition, Zn-NCDs could be applied as a new nano fertilizer and technology for in vivo plant imaging.

head.ce:title: Effect of a new slow-release zinc fertilizer based on carbon dots on the zinc concentration, growth indices, and yield in wheat (Triticum aestivum)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study aimed to introduce a new carbon dots nanocarrier (Zn-NCDs) as a slow-release Zn fertilizer. Zn-NCDs was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and characterized by instrumental methods. A greenhouse experiment was then conducted involving two Zn sources (Zn-NCDs and ZnSO4), three concentrations of Zn-NCDs (2, 4, and 8 mg/L), and under sand culture conditions. This study comprehensively evaluated the effects of Zn-NCDs on the zinc, nitrogen, and phytic acid content, biomass, growth indices, and yield in bread wheat (cv. Sirvan). Also, a fluorescence microscope was used to examine the in vivo transport route of Zn-NCDs in wheat organs. Finally, the availability of Zn in soil samples treated with Zn-NCDs was evaluated over 30 days in an incubation experiment. The findings indicated that Zn-NCDs as a slow-release fertilizer increased root-shoot biomass, fertile spikelet, and grain yield by 20, 44, 16, and 43%, respectively, compared to ZnSO4 treatment. The concentration of zinc and nitrogen in the grain was increased by 19% and 118%, respectively, while phytic acid was decreased by 18% than ZnSO4 treatment. Microscopic observations revealed that wheat plants could absorb and transfer Zn-NCDs from roots to stems and leaves through vascular bundles. This study demonstrated for the first time that Zn-NCDs could be used as a slow-release Zn fertilizer with high efficiency and low cost in wheat enrichment. In addition, Zn-NCDs could be applied as a new nano fertilizer and technology for in vivo plant imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: All authors contributed to the study conception and design. Material preparation, data collection and analysis were performed by Mina Alikhani, Atena Mirbolook and Jalal sadeghi. The first draft of the manuscript was written by Mina Alikhani and all authors commented on previous versions of the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

In recent years, zinc deficiency has become a widespread problem in most parts of the world (Khan et al., 2022; Praharaj et al., 2021). Zinc deficiency in the human diet can cause various health issues, such as anemia, skin diseases, sexual immaturity, decreased appetite, and growth in children (Godswill et al., 2020). The population in developing nations is experiencing a shortage of zinc because over 80% of them consume foods that are lacking in adequate zinc nutrition (Abbas et al., 2021).

Enriching plants with zinc is a fast and effective method to boost the amount of zinc in food sources, which can solve the issue of zinc deficiency in the human diet (Cakmak, 2008). A simple and convenient way to enhance zinc levels in plants is to use regular fertilizer (Suganya et al., 2020). Common sources of zinc for plant nutrition include synthetic chelates like Zn-EDTA and Zn-DTPA, as well as ZnSO4 fertilizer (Zhao et al., 2019). Synthetic chelates are a better option for zinc supply compared to free metal cations. Synthetic chelates enable a slow and readily available release of zinc ions, making them a superior choice for plant zinc supply (Souri and Hatamian, 2019).

However, commercial zinc chelates (such as Zn-EDTA) are expensive and their direct or indirect effects on the environment are also becoming a growing concern (Rahale et al., 2021). Hence, the current requirement is to prioritize sustainable agriculture practices and ensure the provision of safe, sufficient, and nutritious agricultural products (Gupta and Seth, 2021).

The use of slow-release fertilizers (SRFs) that provide nutrients gradually has gained significant attention due to their ability to enhance the effectiveness of traditional fertilizers, mitigate environmental issues, reduce nutrient loss, and increase efficacy (Rashid et al., 2021). Currently, researchers have used nanotechnology extensively to produce slow-release fertilizers (Usman et al., 2020). Nanotechnology is mainly used in agriculture to decrease the number of chemicals distributed, limit nutrient losses from fertilizing, and improve crop yields using nutrient and pest management (Van Nguyen et al., 2022). Furthermore, the utilization of nanotechnology enhances agricultural technologies' productivity and safety, consequently boosting their efficiency and acceptance (Zulfiqar et al., 2019).

Numerous nanomaterials have recently been developed for application in agriculture (Kumar and Seth, 2021; Prakashraj et al., 2022). Meanwhile, carbon nanomaterials are one of environmentally friendly components and are widely used for the development of agriculture (Gopinath et al., 2021). Carbon dots (CDs) (Guo et al., 2022; Kou et al., 2021), carbon nanotubes (Patel et al., 2020; Safdar et al., 2022), and fullerenes (Chandel et al., 2022; Khamitova et al., 2019) have all been investigated for their effects on plants, but CDs have gained particular attention due to their small size (less than 10 nm) (Liang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). They are also important due to their numerous advantages, which include simplicity of synthesis and surface modification, high water solubility, fluorescence properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility (Feng et al., 2021). CDs are easily absorbed by plants and can promote growth by increasing photosynthesis, nutrient uptake, and nitrogen fixation (Li et al., 2020). Studies have also shown that CDs have a positive impact on crop plant growth and resistance to environmental stresses at appropriate concentrations (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021).

In this work, carbon dot was used as a new carrier for the slow release of Zn micronutrient. For this purpose, carbon dot doped with zinc (Zn-NCDs) was synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal method and identified by instrumental methods. Then, the effect of the Zn-NCDs as a zinc source was evaluated on the bread wheat (cv. Sirvan) plant under greenhouse conditions. As far as we know, few studies have focused on the effect of CDs on crop quality, while most studies have investigated the effect of CDs on plant growth. Also, there have been no published reports of using CDs as carriers for slow-release of plant micronutrients.

All compounds and salts were obtained from the Sigma Company and utilized without further refinement. Double distilled water was used to make all the solutions. A PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrophotometer was used to record the infrared spectra of KBr pellets at the standard ambient temperature in the 400-4000 cm−1 region. The morphology of Zn-NCDs was evaluated using a ZEISS Company Sigma VP, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) coupled with energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) with preliminary gold coating. UV–Vis analysis was done using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The hydrodynamic radius and zeta potential of Zn-NCDs were measured using a dynamic light scattering (DLS)/zeta analyzer (Zetasizer Malvern Company). A transmission electron microscope (TEM) made by Zeiss was used to investigate the Zn-NCDs structure. Thermo Finnigan Flash 1112 EA was used to measure the element analyses (CHN). Measurements of photoluminescence (PL) were performed using a F-7000 Hitachi spectrophotometer. The amount of Zn doped in NCDs was determined using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) from Spectro Arcoes model-76004555. An atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS) was utilized to measure the amount of zinc in the wheat plant and soil (PerkinElmer 3030, USA). An Olympus BX51 fluorescence microscope was applied to record fluorescent images while being excited by UV and green light.

The Zn-NCDs was synthesized following the procedure previously described by Cheng et al. (2016), with minor modifications. Briefly, citric acid (1 mmol, 0.19 g) and urea (2 mmol, 0.12 g) were dissolved in deionized water (10 ml) and subsequently mixed with various concentrations of zinc chloride (1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3 mmol). After that, the aqueous solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated for 12 h at 200 °C. A dark yellow-colored suspension was obtained at the end of this reaction, which was then cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, this solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min to separate the large particle. The supernatant liquid was dried in an oven at a temperature of 70 °C. Finally, all reactions were repeated three times for statistical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating absorption and transport of Zn-NCDs in plant organs

ce:section.ce:para: Seeds were surface-sterilized with 10% sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) solution, washed five times with deionized water, and equally sown in specific containers containing cocopeat, vermicompost, and perlite. After 10 days, three wheat seedlings were transferred to a plastic container containing a 100 mL solution of Zn-NCDs (with concentration: 8 mg/L). The container was covered and placed in a growth chamber at 25 °C without light. At different times after incubation by wheat (during days 3, 5, and 7), 3 mL of the Zn-NCDs solution was collected from the container and analyzed by PL spectroscopy. The Zn-NCDs solution without incubation by wheat was also investigated as the control under the same condition.

Three wheat seedlings were transferred to a container containing a 100 mL solution of Zn-NCDs with a concentration of 8 mg/L. The container was kept in the greenhouse (Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Iran) under the controlled condition at 25 ± 3 °C temperature, illuminated for 8 h daylight duration for 7 days. After that, wheat seedlings were removed, adequately cleaned with deionized water, and then sliced into pieces. The cuttings of roots, stem, and leaves were examined using an Olympus DP71 fluorescent microscope under UV and green light. Each picture was taken with an exposure period of 6 s. In addition, fluorescent microscope images of plant tissues incubated in water were examined as a control.

The composition of basic nutrition solution included (I) macronutrient: KNO3, 2.0 mM; Ca(NO3)2, 2.0 mM; NH4H2PO4, 1.0 mM; MgSO4.7H2O, 1.0 mM; K2SO4, , 2.0 mM; (II) micronutrients: Fe-EDTA, 63.0 μM; H3BO3, 3.0 μM; MnCl2, 1.0 μM; Cu(NO3)2.6H2O, 1.0 μM; Na2MoO4.2H2O, 1.0 Μm (Hoagland and Arnon, 1950). The zinc level in ZnSO4, Zn-NCDs1, Zn-NCDs2, and Zn-NCDs3 treatments was 2.0, 2.0, 4.0, and 8.0 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the treatment without zinc element was considered a negative control treatment.

The research was carried out in a greenhouse condition at Ferdowsi University of Mashhad (36°18 36.2″ N 59° 31 48.1″ E) using a completely randomized design with 3 replications and 5 treatments. Treatments consisted of various Zn sources (ZnSO4, Zn-NCDs1, Zn-NCDs2, and Zn-NCDs3) and a treatment without Zn as control.

For this investigation, 40 kg of coarse river sand with uniform particle size (0.7–2.0 mm) was chosen. The sand was first washed and then treated with 20% hydrochloric acid in a “Kee bush” sand digester for 6 h with a continuous supply of steam at a pressure of 20 lbs/sq. inch passed from an electrode boiler. This procedure was performed three times. Following the final hot acid treatment, the sand was washed multiple times with deionized water (Smith et al., 1983).

Subsequently, plastic pots were filled with 2.5 kg of dry-sterilized sand, and four germinated wheat seeds were planted in each pot. Then, according to the water holding capacity of each pot, 200 ml of the nutrient solution was added to each pot. Plants were grown in a greenhouse with a temperature range of 20–25 °C and daily sunlight. Sand humidity of pots was maintained at 70% of the field capacity by weighing the pots daily. Finally, two plants from each pot were harvested after 30 days of growth to measure nitrogen, and zinc concentration in the shoot. Also, the remaining plants were harvested when the seeds reached full maturity (90 days old).

The plants were carefully uprooted and plant tissues (root, shoot, and grain) were manually separated. Shoot and root lengths were measured with a metric scale. Then, the washed tissues were dried in an oven at 65 °C until a constant weight was achieved. After that, the dry weight of root, shoot, and grain yield was determined.

The zinc concentration in the shoot (30 days old) and grain (90 days old) was measured using an atomic absorption spectrophotometer and the dry ashing method. The dried plant samples were ground and heated in an electric furnace at 600 °C for 5 h. Ashes were digested in HCl (2 M) by heating and and diluted to 15 ml with distilled water. Then, zinc concentration in solutions was measured by atomic absorption (Chapman and Pratt, 1961).

Dried shoot and grain were digested in an acidic mixture of HClO4 and H2SO4 (with a ratio of 1:3 V/V) and diluted with distilled water. Total N concentration was determined using Kjeldahl's acid-digestion procedure (Bremner, 1965).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of phytic acid content and phytic acid/zinc mole ratio
ce:section.ce:para:
The phytic acid was measured according to the method described by Bassiri and Nahapetian (1977). Grain samples were finely milled and the amount of phytic acid in the samples was measured using the ion exchange method. The phytic acid quantitative measurements were accomplished by the molybdate assay (Ames, 1966). In summary, 6 mL ammonium molybdate solution, 1 mL 1% sulfuric acid, and 5 mL diluted sample solution were mixed. After 30 min of incubation at 60 °C, the absorbance was measured at 420 nm. Also, the phosphorus standard curve was generated using standard phosphate solutions with concentrations of 40, 80, 240, and 400 μg/ml. The following equation was used to calculate the amount of phytic acid (in mg g−1) (Najafi et al., 2012):

Based on WHO, the phytic acid to zinc mole ratio is an excellent indicator of zinc bioavailability (Lian et al., 2023). Equation (2) was used to calculate the mole ratio of phytic acid to zinc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the zinc release from the Zn-NCDs in the soil
ce:section.ce:para: A soil incubation experiment was carried out to determine slow-released behavior of Zn-NCDs. The experiment used a completely randomized design (CRD) with three replications and two treatments (ZnSO4 and Zn-NCDs). The initial properties of the soil are shown in Table 1 .

For this purpose, 0.2 mg of various Zn fertilizers were added to 100 grams of soil in plastic cups. Using distilled water, the soil moisture content was kept at 70% of the field capacity throughout the experiment. The prepared samples were stored in the dark and under 25 °C. Afterward, the soil samples were collected at five-day intervals (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 days) and dried in the air. The available zinc concentration was extracted by the DTPA-TEA method and determined by atomic absorption (Lindsay and Norvell, 1978).

Data were submitted for analysis of variance (One-way ANOVA). The effects of the fertilizer type and their relationships were evaluated using general linear models (GLM). To compare means, the Duncan multiple range tests were employed (P < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the molar ratio of zinc in the preparation of Zn-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal molar ratio of zinc, different zinc molar ratios were reacted with citric acid and urea. Elemental analysis (CHN) showed the exact percentage of C, H, and N elements while the ICP results showed the percentage of zinc elements in Zn-NCDs (Table 2 ). The results revealed that with increasing the molar ratio of zinc element, the percentage of C element decreased. Zn-NCDs with 3 molar ratio of zinc had nearly 4% of C element. As a result, carbon dots doped with zinc in a molar ratio of 1 was used in the following steps.

The FT-IR spectrum of the Zn-NCDs sample was taken to study the chemical structure and type of bonds (Fig. 1 ). The peaks at 3429, 3216, and 2958 cm−1 correspond to hydroxyl group stretching vibrations (ν OH), asymmetric amine stretching vibrations (νasymNH2), and asymmetric stretching vibrations (νasym CH2), respectively (Atchudan et al., 2020). Furthermore, the peaks at 1765, 1622, and 1138 cm−1 confirm the presence of functional groups CO, CN, and C–N. The peak at 690 cm−1 indicates the connection of zinc metal to carbon dots via the Zn–O bond (Tammina et al., 2020).

The optical properties of Zn-NCDs are shown in Fig. S1. At a very low concentration (0.2 mg/mL), the Zn-NCDs exhibited high fluorescence and a bright blue colour under a UV lamp (Fig. S1a). The main peak at 280 nm and a weak shoulder at 370 nm were observed in the UV–Vis spectrum, which are related to π→π* transitions of sp2 aromatic carbons and n→π* transitions of nitrogen or carbonyl groups in NCDs, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2020). A peak with a narrow distribution and high intensity was observed in the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm and the emission wavelength of 514 nm (Fig. S1b) (Chen et al., 2022).

The morphology of Zn-NCDs was confirmed by examining the images of FESEM and TEM. The FESEM image shows that zinc chloride (white particles) is well attached to NCDs carbon nanoparticles (porous part of the image) (Fig. S2). Also, the TEM image of Zn-NCDs shows spherical particles with uniform dispersion and no apparent aggregation (Fig. 2 a) (He et al., 2022). The average particle size was obtained by drawing the particle size histogram, approximately 4 nm (Fig. 2b).

EDS, CHN, and ICP-OES analyses were performed to ensure the chemical composition and binding of zinc on carbon dots (NCDs). The presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and zinc elements in the structure of Zn-NCDs was confirmed by EDS analysis (Fig. S3). The gold peak in the EDS spectrum was caused by the gold nanometer layer deposited on the Zn-NCDs sample for FESEM investigation (Fratoddi et al., 2021). Finally, the exact percentages of carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen elements were determined using CHN analysis, and the percentage of zinc element was determined using ICP analysis, the results are shown in Table 2. All studies showed that the zinc element was successfully attached to NCDs and the Zn-NCDs were synthesized.

Fig. S4 a shows the values of average hydrodynamic diameter for the Zn-NCDs nanoparticles obtained by DLS. The average hydrodynamic diameter (17.35 nm) was greater than the average size of Zn-NCDs determined by TEM imaging (Fig. 2b). In general, the hydrodynamic diameter of a particle in a solvent is larger than the particle size measured in vacuum. In an aqueous environment, the formation of a hydrated layer on the surfaces of Zn-NCDs leads to an increase in particle size (Yao et al., 2018). At pH = 7, the prepared Zn-NCDs had a zeta potential of −21.99 mV (Fig. S4b). This negative value is most likely due to the presence of carboxylate (COO−) and chloride (Cl−) functional groups on the surface of Zn-NCDs. The value of zeta potential determines whether carbon dots are stable in the short or long term. Carbon dots with a high zeta potential (negative or positive) are considered electrostatically stable, whereas particles with a low zeta potential coagulate or aggregate quickly (Shishodia et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zn-NCDs uptake and transfer in plant organs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high fluorescence of Zn-NCDs, photoluminescence (PL) measurements were taken to demonstrate how long the wheat seedlings could absorb Zn-NCDs from the solution. Fig. 3 b shows that Zn-NCDs dispersed in water exhibit fluorescence stability and the intensity of fluorescence emission rarely varied. On the other hand, the fluorescence emission intensity of Zn-NCDs when incubated with wheat seedlings decreased with time due to the continuous uptake of Zn-NCDs by the plant over a 7-day incubation period (Fig. 3a).

Fluorescence microscopy images were taken on the seventh day to study the absorption and translocation conditions of Zn-NCDs in the wheat seedlings. Bright blue (Fig. 4 a) and bright yellow (Fig. 4b) emissions were observed in slices of roots, stem, and leaves excited by UV and green light, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. S5 depicts the fluorescence image of Zn-NCDs powder under UV light excitation, which shows that these particles appear as blue-glow particles under UV light. Moreover, no fluorescence emission was observed in roots, stem, and leaves parts cultured with pure water as a control (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Zn-NCDs on growth indices, morphological parameters and grain yield

ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 shows the effect of zinc fertilizers on the morphological parameters of wheat. According to the findings, Zn-NCDs fertilizer had a greater effect on increasing growth and yield indicators than ZnSO4 fertilizer. The length of the spikes was not significantly different among the zinc treatments when the treatments were compared (Fig. 5b). The usage of Zn-NCDs fertilizer in comparison to ZnSO4 resulted in a significant increase in the number of spikes. In the application of Zn-NCDs1, the fertile spikelet had the highest amount (55.4% and 16.51% more than control and ZnSO4, respectively). In addition, compared to the ZnSO4 treatment, the number of grains per spike increased by 14.30% in the Zn-NCDs1 treatment. Furthermore, the weight of 1000 grains in the Zn-NCDs1 treatment was 36.33% higher than ZnSO4 treatment. Furthermore, there was no significant difference between Zn-NCDs treatments in most of the morphological parameters.

The use of zinc fertilizers significantly increased grain yield in wheat compared to control (Table 4). The highest grain yield was obtained in plants supplied by Zn-NCDs1. The results showed that the application of Zn-NCDs1 increased the grain yield by 43.08% compared to ZnSO4. Plants fed Zn-NCDs1 produced the most grain yield, followed by Zn-NCDs3 and Zn-NCDs2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zinc and nitrogen content in shoot and grain

ce:section.ce:para: Table 5 shows the average zinc concentration in wheat shoots and grains. The findings indicate that Zn-NCDs can provide a sufficient amount of zinc for plant absorption. The highest zinc concentration was found in Zn-NCDs treatments (Table 5). Zinc concentration in grains treated with Zn-NCDs1 was 18.67 times higher than the zinc concentration in grains treated with ZnSO4. The amount of zinc in the grain enhanced significantly as Zn-NCDs concentration was increased. Hence, the grains treated with Zn-NCDs3 had the highest zinc concentrations.

The percentage of total nitrogen in the shoot (on the 30th day) and grain (on the 90th day) significantly increased in Zn-NCDs treatments (Table 5). Accordingly, Zn-NCDs1 increased the nitrogen content in grains by 118.24% compared to ZnSO4. Among the Zn-NCDs treatments, the Zn-NCDs3 treatment had the highest percentage of nitrogen in the shoot (5.97%) and grain (5.30%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of phytic acid and the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc in grain
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the effects of various zinc fertilizers on phytic acid content and the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc in grain. The highest concentration of phytic acid and the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc were found in grains with the lowest concentration of zinc. The Zn-NCDs1 treatment had the lowest concentration of phytic acid, which reduced the amount of phytic acid by 45.20% in comparison to the control (Fig. 6a). On the other hand, the concentration of phytic acid in Zn-NCDs treatments was not significantly different. The molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc in wheat grains was more than 16 in control treatment, but decreased below 6 in ZnSO4 treatment, and less than 4 in Zn-NCDs treatments (Fig. 6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of zinc release from Zn-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mutual relationship between various metal-containing compounds and soil reveals more information about their potential in soil application (Marrugo-Negrete et al., 2017); thus, zinc release from ZnSO4 and Zn-NCDs fertilizers in soil was investigated. Fig. 7 displays patterns of available zinc release from Zn-NCDs and ZnSO4 fertilizers in the soil. The data showed that after 15 days, the total available zinc was released from the ZnSO4 fertilizer, and the zinc concentration decreased below the detectable limit. The Zn2+ release from Zn-NCDs was rapid for the first 5 days, then slowed and continued for another 25 days, reaching a concentration of 1.35 mg/kg. As a result, the findings suggest that using carbon nanocarrier (NCDs) helps to slow down zinc ion release.
This study aimed to develop and identify a new slow-release zinc fertilizer using a carbon dots nanocarrier (Zn-NCDs). This fertilizer was prepared using a simple, one-step, and low-cost method.

The sand culture results revealed that the application of Zn-NCDs compared to ZnSO4 led to the increase of zinc concentration in the shoot and grain of wheat plants (Table 5). This carbon nanocarrier is significantly smaller in size than ZnSO4. Nanoparticles are taken into the cell by a transporter during plant metabolism and can easily pass through the cell wall due to the limited size of cell pores (Dietz and Herth, 2011).

Furthermore, the surface charge of Zn-NCDs is negative (-21.99 mV) due to the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on its surface. As a result, the electrostatic repulsive interactions between the negatively charged polysaccharide on the root surface and the negatively charged functional groups on the Zn-NCDs enhanced the transport of the Zn-NCDs from the root to the shoot of the plants via the xylem.

Since Zn-NCDs have a strong fluorescence property, the fluorescence microscope was utilized to track the absorption path of the Zn-NCDs in the wheat plant. Fluorescence microscope images revealed significant blue and yellow fluorescence emission in xylems (Fig. 4). Therefore, the Zn-NCDs were transferred from root to leaf by vascular bundles (Gong and Dong, 2021).

In this study, the mean concentration of zinc in wheat grains in the application of Zn-NCDs1, Zn-NCDs2 and Zn-NCDs3 was 19.7 mg/kg, 25.2 mg/kg and 38.3 mg/kg, respectively. These values are within the range reported by Malakouti et al. (1999), which is 9–24 mg/kg zinc in various wheat flours consumed in Iran. Furthermore, Gargari et al. (2007) reported 14 mg/kg of zinc in milled wheat consumed in Tabriz, Iran. It is well known that the zinc concentration in wheat grain is inadequate to indicate zinc bioavailability in the diet of humans. The amount of phytic acid existing in the grain is also important because it can reduce zinc bioavailability by forming a complex with zinc. According to the report of Reinhold (1971), zinc deficiency in most regions of Iran is caused by high levels of phytic acid in people's staple foods such as bread. In this study, the lowest concentrations of phytic acid were obtained in Zn-NCDs treatments, ranging from 78 to 67 mg/100 g.

The effect of phytic acid on zinc absorption is proportional to the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc. As a result, the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc is a more suitable indicator for the bioavailability of zinc in wheat grain (Saha et al., 2017). According to the research results, zinc fertilization reduces the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc in zinc-deficient soils, though this ratio varies depending on the genotype of the plant and the time of seed development (Erdal et al., 2002). Based on the results of this study, using Zn-NCDs and ZnSO4 fertilizers reduced the molar ratio of phytic acid. The phytic acid to zinc molar ratios for Zn-NCDs1, Zn-NCDs2, and Zn-NCDs3 were 3.93, 2.78, and 1.73, respectively.

Many studies have been done to investigate the phytic acid to zinc mole ratio. Ghasemi et al. (2013a) investigated the effect of zinc-amino acid chelates on the phytic acid-to-zinc molar ratio in wheat plants. The molar ratios calculated for many plants treated with zinc-amino acid chelates were 5–15 while this ratio in untreated plants was higher than 15. In another study, Seddigh et al. (2016) showed that seed priming with [Zn(Histidine)2] and [Zn(Arginine)2] reduced this ratio to less than 5. Also, Mirbolook et al. (2020) found that using Zn-glycin and Zn-arginine chelates reduced the phytic acid to zinc ratio to less than 7.

For the bioavailability of zinc in the human diet, the World Health Organization proposed the following classification based on the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc: when the molar ratio is greater than 15, zinc bioavailability is only 10–15%; when the molar ratio is 5–15, zinc bioavailability is medium, ranging from 30 to 35%; and when the molar ratio is less than 5, zinc bioavailability is high, ranging from 45 to 55% (Organization, 2002). Therefore, fertilization with Zn-NCDs reduces the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc to an acceptable level.

The outcomes of this section also revealed that the reported value for the molar ratio of phytic acid to zinc in the control treatment was also nearly low (phytic acid/Zn = 16.5). The concentrations of PA and Zn vary between wheat genotypes. Therefore, seeds with a low PA and high zinc content are considered suitable for use in cereal-based foods (Tavajjoh et al., 2011). As a result, this wheat variety (Sirvan) is appropriate in this regard. Additionally, studies on this wheat variety have demonstrated its superior baking qualities (Najafian et al., 2012).

Application of zinc fertilizer is an effective method for improving growth indicators and grain quality (Ramzan et al., 2020). Therefore, we compared the effects of Zn-NCDs on wheat grain growth indices and grain quality compared to ZnSO4 fertilizer using the sand culture method (Tables 3 and 4). The findings of this study demonstrated that fertilizing wheat plants with Zn-NCDs more than ZnSO4 increased growth parameters and grain yield. Zinc activates tryptophan synthetase, which regulates the synthesis of tryptophan, involved in the biosynthesis of indoleacetic acid (IAA) (Castillo-González et al., 2018). IAA plays an important role in the growth and development of plants (Sourati et al., 2022). Furthermore, the simultaneous effect of zinc and nitrogen elements increases the plant's catalytic and metabolic activities (Hafeez et al., 2013). Also, the role of zinc in increasing plant fertility led to an increase in the number of fertile spikes and a decrease in the number of infertile spikes (Das et al., 2019).

The higher nitrogen concentrations in wheat shoots and grains supplied with Zn-NCDs compared to ZnSO4 may be due to the presence of nitrogen groups in the structure of this fertilizer (13.59%) and the nutritional improvement of nitrogen. Plants utilize nitrogen to generate protoplasm and amino acids, which are considered necessary for the formation of plant tissue and plant proteins (Guo et al., 2021). Nitrogen is also required to produce enzymes, which are required to support various biochemical processes throughout the plant's life (Yadav et al., 2022).

The mutual relationship between various metal-containing compounds and soil reveals more information about their potential in soil application (Marrugo-Negrete et al., 2017); thus, zinc release from ZnSO4 and Zn-NCDs fertilizers in soil was investigated. In this study, the zinc release rate from various fertilizers was as follows: ZnSO4> Zn-NCDs. Ghasemi et al. (2013b) realized that complexation reduced compound degradability and allowed compounds to stay longer in the soil. As a result, zinc complexes may play a significant role in zinc mobility and bioavailability in the soil environment. Therefore, Zn-NCDs fertilizer was more effective in the mobility and bioavailability of zinc in the soil environment than ZnSO4. Zn ion release from Zn-NCDs was rapid for the first 5 days, then slowed and continued for another 25 days, reaching a concentration of 1.35 mg/kg. Also, the results showed that after 30 days, almost 1 mg of Zn was released from the nanocarrier (NCDs) to the soil.

Some studies have also revealed information on how nanocarriers affect the slow release of the Zn nutrient. A study by Kumar et al. (2018) showed the use of carbon nanofiber had a positive effect on the slow release of Zn. Their findings revealed that after 30 days, carbon nanofibers released approximately 3 mg of Zn to the soil. Yuvaraj and Subramanian (2018) used nano zeolite for slow release of zinc and the results showed that 3 mg of zinc was released from nano zeolite to the soil after 30 days. In another study, Yuvaraj and Subramanian (2021) also loaded zinc sulfate onto a carbon nanosphere and used this carbon nanocarrier for the slow release of zinc micronutrients. They observed that this nanocarrier was able to release 2.5 mg of zinc to the soil after 25 days.

Comparison with previously published results revealed that NCDs nanocarrier could release zinc micronutrients from its surface more slowly, which was probably due to the small particle size (less than 5 nm) and higher stability of Zn-NCDs. The presence of reactive sites in the structure of NCDs, such as carboxyl, amine, and hydroxyl groups, leads to the formation of a stable complex with zinc ions. Also, carbon dots with particle size less than 5 nm and a high surface area lead to increased nutrient holding capacity, which minimizes excess fertilizer losses (Khayal et al., 2021; Yuvaraj and Subramanian, 2021).

The findings of this study are very encouraging because they demonstrate that Zn-NCDs nano fertilizers can be used as controlled-release fertilizers to release Zn in the soil based on plant needs gradually. The need for expensive fertilizers encapsulated in biosynthetic polymers that release micronutrients gradually can be eliminated by utilizing low-cost materials (such as carbon dots) (Saxena et al., 2014). However, it is important to conduct further soil tests in both greenhouse and field conditions. In addition, various methods of applying zinc fertilizer, such as foliar spraying on different plants, may prove effective for this new type of fertilizer.

Finally, although CDs have made significant progress in this field, more work is still needed to apply CDs in agriculture. The major limitation of the use of CDs in agriculture is their low yield. Also, it is challenging to obtain CDs as a solid powder because of the numerous hydrophilic groups on their surface, which limits the possibilities for long-distance transportation and storage. More research should also be done on the systematic effects of CDs on many biological species, including microbes, aquatic organisms, insects, etc.

According to the study, slow-releasing Zn application through carbon dots nanocarrier significantly increased wheat growth indicators, biomass, grain yield, nitrogen, and zinc content. Furthermore, the phytic acid to zinc molar ratio in wheat grains was significantly reduced, which affects the bioavailability of zinc in wheat grains. The findings also showed that Zn-NCDs had a good in vivo fluorescence imaging effect, which is critical in biological imaging technology. A future research direction would be to assess the effect of CDs nanocarriers on the availability of zinc or other nutrients in wheat plants in the soil environment. Finally, although important progress has been made on the effect of CDs on plants, more efforts and investigations are still needed for the application of CDs in agriculture.
The research leading to these results received funding from the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under Grant Agreement No 99022387.
